title: Canelés bordelais
subtitle: French Bordeaux cakes with a thick caramelised crust and custardy interior.
excerpt: Canelés bordelais are small French cakes from Bordeaux: dark mahogany on the outside with a thick, slightly bitter caramelised crust, and a soft, custardy, almost chewy interior. The batter is made the day before, then baked in small buttered tins at very high heat. The result looks more dramatic than it is to make.
sourceNotes: Canelés are a speciality of Bordeaux, attributed to the Ursuline nuns of the seventeenth century who used egg yolks leftover from wine fining to make a cake for the poor. The distinctive fluted copper moulds are the traditional vessel; the copper conducts heat in a way that creates the characteristic thick crust. Modern silicone canelé moulds are widely available and produce a similar if slightly less dramatic result. Mini muffin tins also work for a home version. The batter is a flavoured crêpe batter, rested overnight to hydrate fully before baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Canelés do not freeze. Eat within four hours of baking; the crust softens overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter must be made the day before (or at least 12 hours ahead) and rested in the fridge overnight. The baked canelés are at their best within four hours.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven, starting at 220°C and reducing to 180°C after 10 minutes; 240°C and 200°C conventional

recipeTools.notes: Or silicone canelé moulds if you have them. Butter the tin thoroughly.

content.text: The batter is made the evening before and left to rest overnight in the fridge. This is not optional; the resting time hydrates the flour fully and lets the gluten relax, which gives the interior its characteristic custardy texture rather than a bready one.

Canelés bake at high heat to develop the crust, then at a lower temperature to cook the interior through. A mini muffin tin works well here; the crust will not be as thick as with proper copper canelé moulds, but the flavour and custardy interior are the same.

Heat {{whole-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} together until the butter melts and the milk is just below a simmer. Remove from the heat and leave to cool to warm (about 40°C), not hot.

Sift {{plain-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a large bowl. Whisk {{eggs}} and {{egg-yolks}} together, then stir into the flour mixture. Gradually add the warm milk, whisking constantly to a smooth, thin batter with no lumps. Stir in {{rum-dark}} if using.
Pass through a sieve if there are any lumps. Cover and refrigerate overnight, or for at least 12 hours.
Heat the oven to 220°C fan (240°C conventional). Butter the mini muffin tin or canelé moulds very thoroughly with softened butter, reaching every surface.
Stir the batter to recombine (it will have separated). Pour into the prepared moulds, filling each to about three quarters full. The batter will expand as it bakes.

Bake at 220°C fan for 10 minutes, then reduce the temperature to 180°C fan (200°C conventional) and bake for a further 45 to 50 minutes. The canelés should be a deep mahogany brown all over, including the tops. Pale canelés are undercooked.
Unmould immediately onto a rack. They will be soft inside when hot; they firm up as they cool. Eat within four hours.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Getting the colour right is the main challenge.

items.cause: Underbaked, or batter not rested overnight.
items.fix: They need to be very dark. A light-coloured canelé is not done. Return to the oven for 10 to 15 minutes. Ensure the batter was rested for at least 12 hours.
Use a generous amount of softened (not melted) butter and coat every surface. Silicone moulds release more easily than metal.
